title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
author: James Silberman
orientation: Silberman argues that the 2020 June Medical Services v. Russo decision exposes the futility of trusting Republican-appointed Supreme Court justices to overturn Roe and calls pro-lifers to abandon judicial supremacy and incrementalism in favor of state-level nullification and the immediate abolition of abortion.
audiences: Abolitionists and historians of the antislavery movement
Republican voters who trust the judicial appointment strategy

questions.question: Why does Silberman argue that the makeup of the Supreme Court disproves the pro-life strategy of relying on Republican judicial appointments?
questions.audience: Republican voters who trust the judicial appointment strategy
questions.answer: Because every decision mandating legal abortion has come from courts dominated by Republican appointees, including the 1992 Casey court that consisted of eight Republican appointees and a pro-life Democrat dissenter from Roe, yet still upheld legal abortion in full.
questions.quote: These decisions to mandate that the lethal brutalization of preborn humans be allowed in every state have come exclusively from courts made up of a majority of Republican appointees.

questions.question: What does the June v. Russo lineup reveal about the willingness of current justices besides Thomas to overturn Roe?
questions.answer: Although Roberts, Alito, Gorsuch, and Kavanaugh have endorsed regulations within the Roe/Casey framework, none have joined Thomas's separate calls to overturn Roe, and Silberman believes hoping for five such votes any time soon is delusional.
questions.quote: Optimism for five such votes any time soon is likely a delusion.

questions.question: How does Silberman frame the upside of the June v. Russo loss for the abolitionist cause?
questions.audience: Abolitionists and historians of the antislavery movement
questions.answer: He sees it as potentially the best thing that could happen to the cause of equal protection for the preborn if it finally pushes pro-lifers to abandon judicial supremacy and incrementalism.
questions.quote: The court's *June v. Russo* opinion has the potential to be the best thing that ever happened to the cause of equal justice and protection for preborn humans if it shakes pro-lifers free from judicial supremacy and incrementalism.

questions.question: What strategic alternative does Josh Craddock point to in the wake of the June v. Russo decision?
questions.answer: Craddock points to bills like the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, a measure of total and immediate abolition, and rebukes National Right to Life for opposing it.
questions.quote: Craddock then points to bills like the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act (a bill of total and immediate abolition) as the solution and rebukes National Right to Life for their opposition to it.

questions.question: Why does Silberman insist that submitting to Roe is sinful, not merely unwise?
questions.answer: Because Roe decrees the mass killing of preborn humans, and no human authority can lawfully bind the conscience to such an order; obedience to it constitutes participation in murder.
questions.quote: Such a decree can never, in the true sense, be legally binding, and submission to such a decree is a sin.

questions.question: How does Silberman use the Fugitive Slave Act as a rhetorical test for pro-life lawyers?
questions.answer: He asks whether it was illegal or immoral for northern states to nullify the Fugitive Slave Act; the lawyers dodge the question because they know the underground railroad and the state nullifications protecting it were righteous.
questions.quote: Pro-life judicial supremacists will often not give an answer because they know the underground railroad was a righteous cause and that state-level nullification to defend the underground railroad from an evil federal law was a righteous cause.

questions.question: What constitutional argument does Silberman make for state-level protection of preborn persons?
questions.answer: He argues state magistrates are bound by the 14th Amendment's equal-protection guarantee, and that creating a category of human non-persons defeats the entire purpose of the amendment.
questions.quote: If "person" as used in the 14th amendment does not include all human beings, it provides no guarantee to anyone.

questions.question: What lexical evidence does Craddock cite to support reading "person" in the 14th Amendment as inclusive of preborn humans?
questions.answer: He shows that 19th-century dictionaries, including Webster's 1864 edition, defined "person" as a living human being without referencing birth, making the terms "person," "human being," and "man" largely interchangeable.
questions.quote: No dictionary of the era referenced birth or the status of being born in its definition of 'person,' 'man,' or 'human being.'

questions.question: What consequence does Silberman say should follow for officials who enforce abortion laws while sworn to the Constitution?
questions.answer: He calls them hypocrites who should be voted out or impeached for moral turpitude and assault on the Constitution.
questions.quote: Anyone who does so should be voted out or impeached for moral turpitude and assault on the constitution.

questions.question: How does Silberman invoke William Lloyd Garrison to discredit pro-life incrementalism?
questions.audience: Abolitionists and historians of the antislavery movement
questions.answer: He cites Garrison's 1833 Declaration of Sentiments, which condemned any scheme that substituted gradualism for the immediate and total abolition of slavery, as the model for rejecting pro-life compromise.
questions.quote: We regard as delusive, cruel, and dangerous, any scheme of expatriation which pretends to aid, either directly or indirectly, in the emancipation of the slaves, or to be a substitute for the immediate and total abolition of slavery.

questions.question: What concrete Texas case does Silberman use to illustrate how incrementalism functionally protects abortion?
questions.answer: He recounts how Rep. Jeff Leach buried the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act in committee and then filed the largely empty Born-Alive Infant Protection Act, earning awards and pro-life accolades while abortion remained legal in Texas.
questions.quote: That is how abortion was kept legal in Texas.

questions.question: Why does Silberman argue that incremental compromise bills actively delay abolition?
questions.answer: Because they let politicians appease moral outrage with cosmetic wins, fundraise off them, and quiet the public demand that would otherwise force real abolition.
questions.quote: It's a cozy arrangement between pro-life politicians and pro-life lobbyists where they reward each other for preventing abolition and then use their "victories" to fundraise and solicit votes.

questions.question: How does Elizabeth Heyrick characterize the moral effect of gradualism on the conscience of a society?
questions.audience: Abolitionists and historians of the antislavery movement
questions.answer: Heyrick argues that the contemplation of gradual emancipation breeds gradual indifference to emancipation itself, allowing even the wise and good to tolerate atrocity by habit and familiarity.
questions.quote: He knew very well, that the contemplation of a gradual emancipation, would beget a gradual indifference to emancipation itself.

questions.question: What is Silberman's posture toward pro-life leaders who continue to oppose immediate abolition bills?
questions.answer: He calls on those who have stayed silent to publicly and repeatedly rebuke National Right to Life and other pro-life groups fighting state abolition bills until pro-life submission to Roe ends.
questions.quote: With Craddock and the abolitionists, deliver a well-deserved rebuke to those pro-life leaders opposing the immediate abolition of abortion.

questions.question: What does Silberman believe should have happened immediately after Roe was decided?
questions.answer: He says Roe should have been defied in every state within weeks of January 22, 1973, and that delayed obedience to God is preferable to continued submission that prolongs the killing of preborn children.
questions.quote: *Roe* should have been defied in every state within weeks of January 22, 1973.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
related.reason: Directly cited in this article as the source for the Fugitive Slave Act analogy used to expose pro-life lawyers' inconsistency on state nullification.

related.title: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Façade
related.reason: Linked from this article to substantiate the claim that the Born-Alive Infant Protection Act Leach passed instead of the abolition bill does virtually nothing.

related.title: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
related.reason: Provides the deeper backstory on the Texas case Silberman cites as the prototype of incrementalist betrayal.

related.title: Four Problems With David Barton's Incrementalism Apologia
related.reason: Tagged with June v. Russo and Judicial supremacy and engages the same incrementalism-versus-abolitionism debate from a complementary angle.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: "We all know nullification doesn't happen in reality."
related.reason: Covers the same Idaho HB361 abolition bill and the pro-life resistance to nullification that Silberman condemns.

related.title: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
related.reason: Develops the judicial-supremacy critique against the same Texas pro-life establishment Silberman names here.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: Movement-level explainer on the constitutional and historical case for state nullification, including Heyrick and the Fugitive Slave Act parallels Silberman invokes.
